pageInline.title: Jaime Hyland
pageInline.tagline: Full-stack developer — Django & Next.js with i18n
pageInline.description: I'm a full-stack developer with a background in languages and education who enjoys building clean, attractive and scalable web apps that inform, educate, increase productivity and improve quality of life.
pageInline.buttonLabel: Looking for a collaborator or developer?

techsSection.heading: Some of the technologies I work with

html5.title: HTML5
html5.description: HTML is the standard markup language used universally to create and structure content on the web. HTML5 is the latest version, adding semantic elements, enhanced graphics support, and improved data and validation features for apps.

css3.title: CSS3
css3.description: The style sheet language used to control how web content is displayed, arranged and visually styled. Correctly leveraged, it enables responsive layouts, visual effects and consistent appearance across pages.

bootstrap.title: Bootstrap
bootstrap.description: Bootstrap provides prebuilt responsive components (buttons, modals, navbars, etc.) and a standard grid system for rapid prototyping. Compared to raw CSS, it helps build interfaces faster but is less flexible for highly customized designs.

tailwind.title: Tailwind CSS
tailwind.description: Tailwind is a utility-first CSS framework for highly customizable designs. Compared to raw CSS, it speeds development and enforces consistency. It’s more flexible than Bootstrap, but lacks Bootstrap’s very speedy ready-made grid system.

javascript.title: JavaScript
javascript.description: JavaScript is the most commonly used programming language for creating website frontends together with HTML and CSS. It's used both to access and manipulate data and the DOM, and to modify HTML and CSS code.

typescript.title: TypeScript
typescript.description: TypeScript is a superset of JavaScript. It enforces stricter typing, provides autocomplete, IntelliSense, and smart hints, making it very useful for writing, debugging, and refactoring code. I use it pretty intensively together with Next.js and React when building and maintaining this site.

react.title: React.js
react.description: React.js is a widely-used JavaScript library for building interactive user interfaces. It has an architecture based on reusable components and supports state management, making my creation of fairly complex UIs manageable. I've mainly used it to build modals, responsive buttons and other dynamic controls.

nextjs.title: Next.js
nextjs.description: Next.js is a React framework for building fast, production-ready web applications. I've found its division of tasks into server-side and client-side tasks, native React integration, simple SEO integration (for single-locale sites  at least) and relatively shallow learning curve particularly useful at this stage of my developer journey.

flutter.title: Flutter
flutter.description: Flutter is a part of the Google stable of open-source UI frameworks for building web apps using a single code base for both iOS and Android-based systems, and even desktop/laptop computers using macOS, Windows or Linux.  The programming language used within it is Dart (also a Google creation).  It provides a variety of UI widgets. In development, it's hot reload features make it easier and faster for developers to test and troubleshoot their work. It's cross-platform consistency as compared to Meta's React Native is one of its more attractive qualities. Compared to React Native it's a steeper learning curve for programmers already familiar with React.js and/or Typescript.

dart.title: Dart
dart.description: Used with Flutter for cross-platform mobile development, Dart is a modern, class-based language inspired by JavaScript, Java, and C#. It compiles to native code for fast, smooth apps on iOS and Android, or to JavaScript for the web, and offers strong typing and powerful async support. Its hot reload feature saves waiting-around time time, making the development experience faster and less frustrating.

python.title: Python
python.description: I use Python mainly in Django coding, in Flask and for scripting.

django.title: Django
django.description: Django is a high-level Python web framework that facilitates startlingly rapid development and clean, pragmatic design. I have used it both purely as a backend (with Next.js or Flutter on the frontend, PostgreSQL at the rear, and RESTful APIs to bridge back and frontend), and also as a fullstack solution, using it to build everything to the user interface using Django templates, with the help of React, Bootstrap, and other tools.

flask.title: Flask
flask.description: Flask is a lightweight Python web framework without any built-in object-relational mapping (ORM), admin panel, or templating system. I dabbled in it during hackathons to help create RESTful APIs.

nodejs.title: Node.js
nodejs.description: Node.js is a runtime for tools and servers that allows you to use JavaScript elsewhere than directly on a browser. Using google's V8 engine, the same engine as in Google Chrome, it comes as part of the Next.js package. Among other things, it allows you develop website backends coding in JavaScript. It's worth noting that Node.js provides the harness within which many web frameworks aside from Next.js do their thing. It's also an essential toolkit when building stuff within the React.js ecosystem.  Its greatest recent relevance for me has been in rewriting GET and POST requests to get around browser restrictions on cross-origin restrictions enforced by modern browsers when creating a fullstack app with a Next.js frontend and a Django backend, both in development and after deployment.

postgresql.title: PostgreSQL
postgresql.description: PostgreSQL is a powerful, open-source relational database. I use it (via Supabase) as the primary database for Django apps, analytics, and structured data storage.

mysql.title: MySQL
mysql.description: MySQL is a widely used relational database with convenient desktop tools and straightforward setup. While it can handle most of what I require of it, I prefer PostgreSQL for projects involving a Django backend because it's more fully integrated with the framework.

heroku.title: Heroku
heroku.description: I usually deploy Django applications on Heroku to leverage its simple workflow and integrated PostgreSQL support. Heroku’s choice of various dynos handle traffic scaling easily, and using Redis to containerize processes (e.g., instant messaging) is straightforward. However, it has hard slug-size limits and requires extra setup for static files (I typically use AWS, though for smaller Django setups Django-Whitenoise does the job nicely).

vercel.title: Vercel
vercel.description: I deploy Next.js apps on Vercel, the platform built by the creators of Next.js, so deployment is very seamless and integrates naturally with server‑side rendering, static site generation and its global CDN. Static files are easy to serve compared to Heroku. Vercel also allows straightforward edge functions and scaling, but doesn’t handle Django backends well. It has upload limits (e.g., ~100 MB on Hobby, ~1 GB on Pro), so very large build artifacts may need splitting or external storage.

aws.title: AWS
aws.description: I use AWS to store and serve static files for my more complex Django apps on hosted Heroku, using S3 for storage and IAM for secure access control.

cloudinary.title: Cloudinary
cloudinary.description: Cloud-based media management for storing, transforming, and delivering images and video via API. While I used it in earlier projects; its new credit-based pricing model has put me off it a little. If I need to use something like it in the future, I'll consider my options.

godaddy.title: GoDaddy
godaddy.description: I use GoDaddy for DNS, hosting, and email (@language-landscapes.com). The service works, but it's costly, outdated, and sometimes inconsistent, which is why I plan to migrate away when I have the time and mental space to deal with the complications such a migration will bring.

stripe.title: Stripe
stripe.description: Integrated Stripe’s payment API to implement a test checkout flow using webhook-based event handling. Setup was straightforward with the support of clear documentation and a transparent developer dashboard.

swagger.title: Swagger
swagger.description: Used Swagger to generate interactive API documentation for Django endpoints; this helped ensure that APIs continued to work correctly and stayed consistent as data models grew more complex.

vscode.title: Visual Studio Code
vscode.description: VS Code is my primary IDE for web development. I used for Django, Next.js, and general front- and back-end coding.

androidStudio.title: Android Studio
androidStudio.description: I use Android Studio exclusively for Flutter development, including emulators and physical devices. Similar to VS Code, but Dart’s declarative style yields different results.

eclipse.title: Eclipse
eclipse.description: I've used Eclipse in the past for Java and C++ coding, especially on Swing components (Java) and MFC UI code (C++). Not currently in use.

github.title: GitHub
github.description: Like nearly everyone else, I use GitHub for version control, repository management, CI/CD integration, using GitHub's in-built Backlog/Kanban project framework. Here is where I manage PRs, issues, and collaborate with teammates on hackathons and where I protect my projects from my mistakes.

git.title: Git
git.description: I'm increasingly proficient with branching, merging and pull requests from the command line as well as conflict resolution across multiple projects. Back in the olden days, I also have Subversion under my belt. Here too, my hackathon experience has helped a lot to develop my change control skills.

jira.title: Jira
jira.description: I used Agile boards and sprint planning in the past to track tasks, manage team workflows, and coordinate project milestones. i'm not using it at present.

jupyter.title: Jupyter
jupyter.description: I have some experience using Jupyter interactive notebooks for Python-based data exploration, analysis, and visualization. My workflows don't include Jupyter at the moment.

java.title: Java Swing
java.description: Java Swing is a framework for building desktop graphical user interfaces. I used it on one occasion to create a simple desktop app to allow an admin to maintain data in a MySQL database. Earlier, I had to dabble in it to adapt complex UIs to support locales other than the original German.

cpp.title: C++
cpp.description: I first encountered C++ during a three-month course, where I became acquainted with some of its basic features. I gained exposure to its benefits as a high-performance development language offering fine-grained control over memory. Later, I dabbled in it while troubleshooting stubborn internationalization issues.

dotnet.title: Visual Basic .NET
dotnet.description: VB .NET is an object-oriented language on the .NET framework. I used it primarily to manipulate the Passolo object model, automating localization tasks and for checking source and localized strings for prohibited substrings or unlocalizable concatenations.

ant_maven.title: Ant & Maven
ant_maven.description: Used for Java build automation and dependency management in earlier projects. Highlights how modern Python/Django and Node/Next.js tooling have simplified this process.

educationSection.heading: Some of the places I've been doing my learning
educationSection.fasModalContent: FÁS was the Irish National Vocational Training Authority at the time I did my foundational training in IT there. It has since been replaced by <link>Solas</link>.
educationSection.trinityModalContent: I studied Philosophy (or "Mental and Moral Science", as the degree course is still called) at <link>Trinity College Dublin</link>. Back in ancient history (1984 to 1988), I did quite a lot of work there on philosophy of logic and mathematics.
educationSection.codeInstituteModalContent:
I completed an online <link>full-stack software development diploma</link> course at the excellent Dublin-based educator Code Institute from 2023 to the end of 2024.
The course put a particular emphasis on the Python language for the modern web, leveraging the Django and Bootstrap frameworks.
My specialized final portfolio project was an e-Commerce site, using Stripe as its online payment system.
educationSection.khanModalContent: <link>Khan Academy</link> is an excellent resource for IT training and Maths education. I've used it so far in self-training in Computer Science Theory, JavaScript programming, Statistics and Calculus.
educationSection.codefinityModalContent: <link>Codefinity</link> is yet another excellent resource for IT training. So far, I've been using it for a variety of learning materials, with a special emphasis on Python.
educationSection.brilliantModalContent: <link>Brilliant</link> is my go-to place when I want to take a short, but productive break. I've been using it so far mainly to revise my old school general maths, and to learn a little about electrical circuits, which has been a closed book to me all my life.
educationSection.kodekloudModalContent:
I've been using <link>KodeKloud</link> on and off to improve my as yet fairly primitive understanding of Linux.
It's an excellent provider of training — in DevOps in particular.
educationSection.leetcodeModalContent: I've been using <link>LeetCode</link> mainly to get a grip on some of the common questions that might come up for me when applying for a job as a full-stack web programmer.
educationSection.linkedinModalContent:
<link>LinkedIn Learning</link> provides a wide variety of courses on the whole gamut of professional disciplines that one may need to learn in the IT industry and elsewhere.
It is the product of LinkedIn's purchase of the famous IT training provider Lynda.com.
So far, I've used it mostly to get a grip on Dart/Flutter.
educationSection.freecodecampModalContent:
<link>freecodecamp</link> is my original provider of many of my basic skills on web development.
I've got enormous mileage out of its Certified Full Stack Developer Curriculum (though it's still in Beta testing at time of writing).
